items.correct: Differences in seawater density driven by BOTH temperature (thermo) and salinity (haline) — cold, salty water sinks at high latitudes, driving a global ocean conveyor belt over centuries.
items.misconception: Thermohaline circulation is driven only by temperature differences in the ocean.
items.random_wrong: Thermohaline circulation is driven by the wind blowing across the ocean surface.
items.why: Salinity is the other half of the name and the mechanism. Freshwater influx (from melting ice) reduces salinity and could slow the circulation — a key climate change concern (the AMOC weakening).

items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.correct: Longshore drift carries sediment past a change in coastline direction (e.g. river mouth); deposition continues into deeper water, building a curved depositional landform with a hook.
items.misconception: A spit forms where the river deposits its sediment at the coast.
items.random_wrong: A spit is a small island just off the coast.
items.why: Spits are coastal (longshore drift) deposits, not river deposits. The hook forms where wind shifts the current. Students confuse spits with deltas — different processes, different settings.

items.q: Which is the world's largest rainforest?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Amazon. “Sahara” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Near the Equator. “Near the poles” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 2000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
